specific_location: Pueblo Reservoir. On the south side at the end of a road that disappears at the reservoir's shore. This paved road separates the Reservoir land from the State Wildlife Area.The bird was in a cove in the first canyon to the east, closer to the entrance of the canyon (where it meets the reservoir proper). Mark Yeager called it Peck Creek canyon when I mentioned it to him.
description: The bird had an alcid shape, and was alternately floating and diving. It never flew when in our view. It had a light grey back, short, light-colored bill, and a dark black head with a bright white neck. The most distinctive feature was the bright white patch (hook-shaped) behind its eye, which helped us distinguish it from other possible murrelets and flagged its plumage as nonbreeding.Behaviors: The bird was in view for at least 10 minutes, diving and floating. It's body was low on the water, including its head. It was a fairly cooperative subject to view, diving when we got too close but surfacing not too far away. I think the closest we may have gotten to it was 50 feet (we were in a canoe on Pueblo Reservoir).Call: None heard.Plumage: Nonbreeding
photographer: Photographer: Rick Clawges, Rick Clawges, Rick Clawges, Rick Clawges
